data.title: A Strong Jobs Report May Be Bad News For The Market
data.description:
The market isn’t focused on jobs anymore; it's now focused on oil and inflation.

That is a major shift ahead of March’s non-farm payroll report, which is expected to show improvement following February’s weak print. But even if the labor data rebounds, it may actually make things worse, not better.

A Big Improvement Is Expected

For March, analysts expect non-farm payrolls to rise by 55,000, versus a decline of 92,000 in February. The unemployment rate is expected to remain unchanged at 4.4%, while average hourly earnings rise by just 0.3% m/m, down from 0.4%.

If the February data were a fluke and March comes in as expected or better, it will likely take pressure off the employment side of the Fed's mandate and shift back to inflation, which, given oil's sudden rise, is quickly becoming a more pressing issue in the months ahead.

Inflation Expectations

Inflation expectations have surged, with year-over-year inflation rates expected to rise dramatically to around 3.4% in March, then climb to almost 3.9% by May before easing. However, that is assuming the worst of the oil impacts are behind us. If oil prices continue to rise, the CPI swap market is likely to adjust to higher levels over time.

From Rate Cuts To Rate Hikes?

What this has done is lead the market to exclude rate cuts from its 2026 equation. Currently, Fed Fund Futures for December 2026 are trading at 3.7%, very close to the upper end of the Fed Fund target range, which is between 3.5% and 3.75%. At least at this point, the market sees no rate cuts from the Fed in 2026.

Other measures for the path of monetary policy also suggest that not only will there be no rate cuts in 2026, but there is the potential for rate hikes. The 3-month Treasury 12-month forward rate is currently trading at a 24-basis-point premium over the 3-month Treasury bill. That is the widest the spread has been since the beginning of 2023.

Not surprisingly, a strong jobs report could bolster the case for rate-hike expectations to continue climbing, especially if there is no relief in sight for oil prices. That would also be a very bullish scenario for the dollar index, which is already nearing a breakout.

The Dollar

The index itself is sitting right below resistance at 100.30, but more importantly, it appears to be breaking out of a bull flag pattern. These patterns serve as continuation patterns and suggest that the dollar index may very well break out. In the meantime, the relative strength index is also trending higher, suggesting bullish momentum is building and that a breakout could push the dollar index to around 102.5.

This would be the worst-case scenario for risk assets, as it would only intensify the pressure already being exerted on the group. A strong dollar, coupled with the potential for rate hikes, would mean tighter financial conditions.

It is not exactly clear that a weak job report would be great either, but it might help to alleviate the market's growing fears that the Fed may have to hike rates, and it would also help to take the pressure off the dollar, which, if anything, could keep financial conditions from tightening further and pressuring risk assets even more.

At the moment, this is a very tricky time for the market, and right now, it's all about oil's impact on financial conditions.

data.title: AMD: Helium Shortage Is The Least Of Its Problems
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Advanced Micro Devices, Inc.'s (AMD) supply agreements with OpenAI and Meta (META) don't change the main thesis laid out in the past article: AMD is expensive and most likely has an inferior product compared to NVIDIA (NVDA).

The company stands to benefit from a surge in demand for CPUs. There are whispers that AMD and Intel (INTC) are increasing prices, capitalizing on the CPU shortage. A report by BNP Paribas (BNP:CA) also sees acceleration in CPU demand. However, one can't ignore the new market entrant - ARM (ARM), an industry behemoth that launched its data center CPU product this month.

I remain bearish on the ticker, but caution that the AI infrastructure buildout is the tide that lifts all boats. Even second-tier suppliers such as AMD are expected to report double-digit growth.

What Happened

Iranian missiles have hit natural gas production facilities at Ras Laffan in Qatar, a small, gas-rich nation that accounts for roughly 20% of global LNG exports. The hostilities in the region have also disrupted tankers sailing through the Strait of Hormuz.

Qatar represents roughly a third of the global Helium supply. Helium is used in Semiconductor manufacturing. Without the gas, the market is likely to witness microchip supply disruptions.

It is worth noting that AMD is a fabless company and relies on Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), Samsung (SSNLF), and GlobalFoundries (GFS) for fabrication, manufacturing, and assembly.

AMD doesn't seem to hold enough inventory for a supply shortage scenario. As of December 2025, AMD held $7.9 billion in inventory.

AMD's average gross margin stood at 50% in the past 5 years. This means that, on average, the company marks up its products at about 100%. Thus, the 7.9 billion in inventory can support about $16 billion in sales. That barely covers last year's sales. These dynamics highlight the limits of AMD's ability to rely on its inventory in the case of supply chain disruptions. Depending on the severity of the disruptions, this could derail AMD's growth plans this year, namely the GPU deployments under the Meta and OpenAI contracts.

The Meta Deal

Last month, AMD signed an agreement with Meta for the supply of 6 gigawatts of AMD's Instinct GPUs. This comes on the heels of another major supply agreement with OpenAI, signed last October. Notably, both agreements carry a 6 GW capacity.

These deals have created a supportive narrative for AMD's shares, which have risen roughly 24% since the initial OpenAI deal. However, I don't believe they change the bearish case for AMD.

The discrepancy in the market's reactions to the two deals constitutes the first red flag, in my view.

The two deals have common key covenants. Both are multi-year, multi-generation deployments of up to 6 GW capacity of AMD's Instinct GPUs. When the initial OpenAI deal was announced on October 6th, 2025, AMD's shares closed the day up 24% and continued going up in the following 3 trading days. On the other hand, when the Meta deal was announced on February 24, 2026, shares were up only 8.8% and quickly ceded all these gains in the following few days.

This shows that the market reaction is about the narrative — a deal with a hyperscaler that validates the Instinct GPU. By the second deal, the market has already shifted to "show me the money," bringing us back to the fundamentals - sustainability of growth, market position, and market share - the areas where AMD is still weak.

Why Did OpenAI and Meta Buy AMD's Chips?

AMD's new contracts with Meta and OpenAI came as NVDA continues to struggle to meet demand. Meta cited its efforts to "diversify" its compute infrastructure when announcing the AMD deal last month.

With NVIDIA infrastructure in high demand, even Hopper and much of the 6-year-old Ampere-based products are sold out in the cloud - Colette Kress, NVDA's CFO, Q4'26 Earnings Call.

Meta's comment suggests that the deal with AMD might have strategic motives — an attempt to strengthen and diversify the GPU supply chain beyond NVDA's dominance. If this premise is true, then the deal isn't the inflection point that many AMD investors are hoping it to be.

This is an important step for Meta as we diversify our compute. I expect AMD to be an important partner for many years to come - Mark Zuckerberg, commenting on the AMD/Meta deal last month.

As the adage goes, customers vote with their wallets, and while the Meta and OpenAI deals are AMD's first gigawatt deployments, the chipmaker doesn't seem to be receiving many votes. This could be a sign of a poor product value proposition.

The bullish case on AMD rests on the idea that it catches up with NVDA. The OpenAI and Meta deals gave this narrative a boost. However, looking closer, the deals lose some of their luster.

AMD issued OpenAI and Meta warrants to purchase up to 160 million shares each to sweeten the Instinct GPU sales deal. The warrants have an exercise price of one cent. These warrants vest with GPU deployments in addition to other covenants, including share price appreciation. For perspective, AMD's shares trade at $202/share.

As of January 2026, AMD had 1.63 billion shares outstanding. The dilution impact of both warrants, if/when fully vested and exercised, is 19.6%.

Losing Market Share?

AMD doesn't break down traditional server CPU and AI GPU sales. Both are bundled within the data center segment.

In the Q3'25 earnings call, the Bank of America's (BAC) analyst pressed management for more color on the product mix. Knowing this would have allowed investors to know whether AMD is gaining or losing market share within the AI GPU market. AMD's executives avoided answering the question.

What investors can do is to assess AMD's market share in the broader data center market, including AI and traditional server hardware. That's the disclosure approach of NVDA (NVDA) and Intel (INTC), its two closest competitors.

In FY25, AMD's data center sales stood at $16.6 billion, growing 32% YoY. That's great and all. But when compared to NVDA, this performance loses some of its luster. In FY'26 (the closest comparable year to AMD's FY'25), NVDA reported $194 billion in data center sales, up 68%. That's more than 10x what AMD sold and more than 2x its growth rate.

The good news is that AMD seems to have taken some market share from INTC, which grew its Data Center sales by a mere 5%.

Note: NVDA's fiscal year ends in late January of each year, while AMD's ends in late December. So, the most comparable fiscal years are NVDA'26/AMD'25, NVDA'25/AMD'24, and so on

Data Center Sales (Bn $) NVDA YoY % Change AMD YoY % Change
NVDA'26/AMD'25 $193.7 68% $16.64 32%
NVDA'25/AMD'24 $115.2 142% $12.58 94%
NVDA'24/AMD'23 $ 47.5 217% $6.50 7%
NVDA'23/AMD'22 $15.0 41% $6.04 64%
NVDA'22/AMD'21 $10.6 58% $3.69 119%
NVDA'21/AMD'20 $6.7 $1.69

Product Benchmark Tests

Before crunching the valuation numbers, one needs to put the entire discussion into context by asking why AMD isn't catching up with NVDA. Why would hyperscalers opt to wait months for NVDA deliveries rather than buy AMD's GPUs?

It is possible that the Instinct GPUs offer an inferior value proposition. This contradicts the hypothesis that AMD is catching up. NVDA's chips might be more expensive, but when measured on a price per token per watt basis, there is evidence that NVDA's GPUs might be more efficient and less costly.

The following table shows a snapshot of the (v.5.1) MLPerf test, the industry benchmark for AI accelerators. It shows the token-per-second output of an 8X cluster of B200, MI300X, and MI325X GPUs for inference application on multiple setups. The figures suggest that AMD is still way behind compared to NVDA on key metrics.

The maturity of the CUDA ecosystem and software around NVDA's GPUs has been cited as a reason for its moat and market share, with AMD bulls arguing that AMD's sales might increase as ROCm — AMD's CUDA equivalent — matures. The results above suggest that AMD's problems might extend beyond software.

Valuation

NVDA isn't cheap, but its valuation multiples can drop quickly given its explosive growth. For example, TTM P/E stands at 35x, forward P/E is 20x, while 1-year forward P/E brings the valuation multiple down to 15x (based on NVDA's share price of $168/share and Wall Street EPS consensus estimates of $8.3/share and $11.1/share in FY'27 and FY'28, respectively).

When comparing AMD's valuation with the potential opportunity, one can see why some investors are buying exposure. The argument is that the market is still nascent and AMD can find its niche. This is the main threat to this thesis, as explained below. However, when considering the cyclicality of the sector, a 30x forward P/E valuation multiple isn't particularly attractive, especially when considering that this P/E value is ~50% higher than NVDA.

At the same time, one needs to acknowledge the improvement in the company's valuation on the back of the recent growth. Forward EV/EBITDA stands at 29x. That's not low, but certainly below AMD's historical average. The same goes for its 30x P/E, which, while high, is below the company's 5-year historical average. These dynamics are captured in the company's PEG ratio, which stands at 0.7x below the sector's median.

Risks

The main risks to this bearish thesis are that AMD is a small fish in a big pond. Even a mid-sized AI chip contract by industry standards could be economically significant for AMD, not to mention the impact on the narrative. Also, NVDA's hyperscaler customers seem very adamant about diversifying the AI chip supply chain. AMD is a primary beneficiary of these supply chain management initiatives as the second-largest GPU supplier. Finally, one can imagine how AMD's deployments under the Meta and OpenAI contracts strengthen its ecosystem, allowing it to gain experience in large-scale deployments and showcase its technology. After all, this bearish outlook considers the possibility that these contracts are partly driven by a desire by hyperscalers to diversify the AI GPU supply beyond NVDA. If this premise is false, then AMD's product quality might not be as inferior as this article suggests. On the other hand, if the premise is right, Meta and OpenAI could succeed in their efforts, enhancing the supply chain and, by extension, the competitiveness of AMD's offerings.

Final Thoughts

Meta and OpenAI contracts are small compared to what the industry is spending on NVDA's GPUs. This relative sales size is a reflection of AMD's trailing market position.

NVDA has long struggled to meet demand. A second supplier could alleviate this supply pressure. However, looking at the results of the most recent standardized MLPerf test, NVDA's GPUs produce far more tokens per second compared to those of AMD. Assuming that the test standardizes power consumption, this could mean that NVDA's accelerators produce more tokens per second per watt. In a world where data center power supply is scarce, this is a value proposition that's hard to ignore.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Merck: Aggressive M&A Efforts To Prepare For A Keytruda Patent Cliff
data.description:
Towards the end of the first quarter, Merck (MRK) announced a larger acquisition to ignite some appeal in its pipeline. This was the second-largest deal announced in recent times, as Merck is spending a combined $15 billion to bolster its pipeline to drive growth, certainly as Keytruda is seen losing exclusivity in the foreseeable future.

These M&A moves do not surprise me, after I concluded last summer that Merck was cheap yet was relying too heavily on Keytruda. That conclusion, however, was drawn at just $80 per share, as a 50% return for such a huge pharmaceutical player is a huge achievement, warranting a more cautious stance.

I like that Merck is working on its pipeline, in this case through M&A, yet much higher expectations in recent times make me err on the side of caution. This makes me interested to keep a close eye on the M&A moves and their contribution, yet I see no reason to get involved here.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including recent M&A action, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

Adding to the Pipeline

Merck has reached a $5.7 billion deal to acquire Terns Pharmaceuticals (TERN). With the deal, the company will acquire a clinical-stage oncology business, with its lead candidate, TERN-701, being a potential treatment for patients with chronic myeloid leukemia, designed to increase the oncology pipeline of Merck.

The FDA has granted Orphan Drug Designation for TERN-701 to treat chronic myeloid leukemia. CML is a lifelong disease that can require lifelong treatment, with about 18,000 new patients diagnosed each year in the US alone.

The company expects to write down the acquisition in the second quarter, when it is anticipated to close, resulting in a charge of $5.8 billion, equivalent to $2.35 per share. That is the one-time charge, as the incurred losses from the business and associated interest costs are seen as hurting earnings by $0.17 per share going forward.

This deal is a second, larger deal for Merck here. At the start of the year, the company closed on the purchase of Cidara Therapeutics. This deal was announced in November of last year, coming in at a $9.2 billion price tag. That transaction is designed for Merck to get its hands on CD388, a phase 3 long-acting antiviral that is designed to prevent influenza for individuals with higher risks for complications.

Igniting Growth

In February, Merck reported its 2025 results. Full-year revenues of $65.0 billion were up a percent on the year before. Some 90% of sales are generated from pharmaceutical products, complemented by the animal health business. The company relies on its blockbuster Keytruda, which grew sales by 7% to $31.7 billion, making up nearly half of sales, as the dependency is only on the increase, up to 49% for the year. In fact, Keytruda sales exceeded 50% of sales in the final quarter of the year.

That is a real concern for investors, as Merck is facing a loss of exclusivity on its key drug, Keytruda, in 2028/2029. Non-GAAP earnings rose by 16% to $22.5 billion, resulting in earnings of $8.98 per share. GAAP earnings came in at $7.28 per share, with most of the reconciliation relating to acquisition-related costs.

Despite the slower pace of growth and concerns about the loss of exclusivity, Merck guided for a modest increase in 2026 sales to a range of $65.5-$67.0 billion. Non-GAAP earnings are seen down to $5.00-$5.15 per share, but this includes a $3.65 per share change relating to the purchase of Cidara, with the acquisition price written down from the get-go. If we add this back, earnings are seen between $8.65 and $8.80 per share, actually down a bit on the year before.

The company ended the year with nearly $35 billion in net debt; this number will be increasing to but $50 billion in between the latest two announced deals. That is huge in dollar terms, with reported EBITDA for 2025 seen around $27 billion, making relative leverage ratios still look manageable.

By now the 2.5 billion shares outstanding exchange hands at $120, for a $300 billion market valuation and about $350 billion enterprise valuation. This values the business at just over 5 times sales, and based on adjusted earnings multiples, these are seen at 13-14 times earnings. These are reasonable multiples; however, they are heavily adjusted and rely heavily on Keytruda, as the same (to a lesser extent) goes for a 2.8% dividend yield.

What Now?

Frankly, investors have liked the more aggressive M&A strategy as of late. After shares rallied to the $120s in 2024, shares plunged back to the $80 mark in 2025, driven by concerns about the loss of the exclusivity of key drugs as well as concerns about pricing and political impact. Those concerns were quickly shrugged off in recent times, as a stock trading in the $80s in November has seen solid 50% returns ever since.

This momentum in the share price has been supported by comments from CEO Robert Davis, who raised the commercial revenue opportunity to around $70 billion by the mid-2030s. This is up just a little from the current revenue base, but this number is up $20 billion from the year before. Moreover, it comes as the company expects Keytruda sales to peak at $35 billion in 2028. While Keytruda will officially see a loss of exclusivity in 2028, additional patents likely create an effective shield until 2029.

This has provided strong support to the shares, creating a potential roadmap in which revenues would be stable for years to come, with new drugs offsetting the loss of exclusivity from Keytruda. However, given a current >50% reliance, this is difficult to do, as expectations have risen substantially since the fall, with the market valuation having increased by a hundred billion here. Given all this, I am leaning cautious, also as the company is taking on quite a bit of debt here, although for the good purpose of igniting potential and adding to the pipeline.

While adjusted earnings look reasonable, continued investment and M&A efforts are needed to make all this happen. Furthermore, there are much higher expectations recently, leverage taken on, and dependency on Keytruda, which makes me cautious here at current levels. However, I am keen to keep track of the challenge faced and how it is addressed by Merck, keeping a close eye on pipeline conversion going forward.

data.title: i-80 Gold: Autoclave Dominance Eclipsed By Suffocating Equity Dilution
data.description:
I mark a Hold rating to i-80 Gold Corp. (IAUX) stock under a strict risk/reward state. On the reward side, i-80 Gold holds a tier-one geological portfolio and the irreplaceable Lone Tree Autoclave that is making it an apex asset in Nevada. However, on the risk side, IAUX stock price upside is capped by a large capital structure (841 million base shares + massive derivative/convertible overhangs) and heavy forward margin loads (3% NSR and 15% prepay deliveries). All in all, the $1 billion recapitalization guarantees survival but transfers huge value to creditors. For my Hold on i-80 Gold stock thesis, downside risks include execution failure on the $430 million autoclave refurbishment and hydrological delays at Granite Creek.

Boost Up from the Nevada Bottleneck and Accelerated NPV

In my opinion, i-80 Gold’s forward bullish trend is mostly based on its shift from a price-taking explorer to an apex owner-operator in Nevada. This shift is emerging through the strategic single-seller status on the Lone Tree Autoclave. Refractory ore processing is the main bottleneck in the Great Basin. As of now, i-80 pays much more (expensive) than it should be, $275–$280 per tonne in toll-milling fees to third parties. The board's approval to proceed with the $430 million Lone Tree refurbishment (backed by Hatch Engineering) is a main upside catalyst. Once the 2,268 tonnes-per-day pressure oxidation [POX] facility is commissioned in late-FY2027, i-80 can capture large margin expansion. As a result, this may alter the cutoff grades and underground life-of-mine [LOM] economics at Granite Creek, Archimedes, and Cove (in my opinion).

Moreover, the recent $1 billion+ recapitalization (mainly the $500 million financing package with Franco-Nevada, National Bank, and Macquarie) is a dual-purpose lever. This recapitalization mostly reduces the $175 million legacy debt overhang and is deploying $50 million toward the acceleration of the Mineral Point open pit. Through pulling forward Mineral Point's permitting and infill drilling from FY2028 to FY2026, i-80 Gold is kind of fast-tracking a project that is its largest gold and silver resource. Mineral Point is slated to push i-80 Gold’s Phase 3 production profile beyond 600K ounces per year. Thus, accelerating this asset by 2 years forms a considerable Net Present Value [NPV] pull-forward that may not be priced in by Wall Street, in my opinion.

Alongside this, on the operational side, the geological continuity at Granite Creek’s South Pacific Zone [SPZ] is proving strong as the drilling expands the mineralized envelope beyond structural boundaries. With all that, i-80 Gold already holds operational leverage to the macro gold tape. To clarify more here, Q4-FY2025 gross profit ticks a positive $11.5 million (that is up from a $15.7 million loss in FY2024) based on $3,887/oz realized gold prices. Through Granite Creek scaling to its 30K–40K oz guidance in FY2026 and Archimedes development advances ahead of schedule (680 meters marked by FY2025’s end), i-80 Gold is building a highly self-stabilizing bridge to its FY2028 Phase-1-target of 150K–200K ounces.

Super Stock Dilution, Hydrological Issues, and Forward Margin Loads

Beyond the above positives, the main negative factor dragging down i-80 Gold stock’s intrinsic value is the huge equity dilution needed to live in its development phase. The outstanding share count has scaled up from 298.5 million at FY2023’s end to 841 million by February-FY2026. This per-share value destruction is expanded more by a large derivative overhang. More on how? There are the 204.4 million outstanding warrants and the newly priced $287.5 million in 3.75% Convertible Senior Notes due 2031. As these notes carry an initial conversion price of $1.93 per share, any organic stock price rally can counter a suffocating cap of arbitrage selling and forced conversions. This terminal dilution caps EPS accretion over the long-term, even if the 600K oz Phase 3 targets are ticked.

Apart from that, the cost of the $500 million lifeline heavily mortgages forward margins. The Franco-Nevada NSR (1.5%, stepping up to a brutal 3% in 2031) cuts top-line revenue from all portfolio properties exactly when Phase 2 (300K–400K oz) attains scale. At the same time, the $250 million gold prepay mandates the delivery of 39,978 ounces between FY2028 and FY2030 (up to 15% of total projected output) that is capping i-80 Gold's upside participation in elevated gold environments. On an operational level, Granite Creek counters big geotechnical and hydrological issues. The mine requires the construction of a second, much larger Water Treatment Plant [WTP] in early FY2026 to handle extreme inflows of 3,500–4,000 gallons per minute [gpm]. This groundwater ingress forced i-80 Gold to alter its mine plan, and it is requiring 20% more material to be mined in FY2026 just to recover lost development time compared to the PEA.

Finally, the Lone Tree commissioning timeline carries huge execution risk. The project needs rigorous updates to air quality, water, and mercury abatement permits. Any NEPA/EIS delays will force i-80 Gold to extend its punitive $275-$280/tonne toll-milling contracts beyond FY2027. This can rapidly drain the newly acquired $63.2 million cash reserve and trigger a secondary liquidity crisis.

i-80 Gold Stock Valuation and Technical Analysis

Valuing i-80 Gold stock needs a heavily diluted Sum-of-the-Parts [SOTP] Net Asset Value [NAV] method using a 5% real discount rate (NPV5%, as per 2025 10-K). The Enterprise Value currently sits at $1.32 billion [$1.2 billion mCap + $179 million Debt/Prepays - $63 million cash]. Although the PEAs for Granite Creek, Archimedes, Cove, and Mineral Point mark strong post-tax NPVs at ~$2,175/oz, current spot prices ($3,800/oz) largely boost gross asset value. I am assuming execution of Phase 1 and 2 (350K oz/year by FY2030) at an AISC of ~$1,227/oz, free of any load operating cash flows may tick $900 million annually. However, I subtract the $430 million Lone Tree CapEx, the $250 million prepay liability, and the 3% F-N royalty leakage. Factoring in the terminal fully diluted share count (that will approach 1.35 billion shares assuming full warrant and convertible note, $1.93 strike conversion), the DCF-led target NAV per share compresses to a tight range of $1.55 to $1.65. Therefore, the current stock price of $1.43 mostly reflects the derisking of bankruptcy and offers limited upside.

Furthermore, the technical state of IAUX stock indicates a show-me consolidation phase (transitional) after a macro downtrend in Q1-FY2026. On a weekly basis, closing at $1.43 on extreme volume (93.2 million shares), i-80 Gold stock marked a decisive bullish reversal candle that is defending the $1.18 Fibonacci pivot. Along with that, the RSI has reset to a neutral state (at 49.54) from prior overbought extremes. At the same time, Full Stochastics flags a deeply oversold state (mainly %K at 17.53). This oscillator is initiating a bullish curl that signals seller exhaustion. However, the MACD line (-0.037) is still bearishly divergent, and the IAUX stock price is trapped under the EMA (5-week) (at $1.55) and the EMA (13-week) (at $1.59). In my opinion, a mean-reversion is initiating, but a high-volume breakout above R1 ($1.6) is a must to confirm a stable trend reversal.

What to do with i-80 Gold’s Next Earnings?

For Q1-FY2026 earnings, smart bulls must ignore GAAP net income that will be heavily distorted by non-cash fair value derivative revaluations ($21.5 million impact in Q4). Instead, the focus must be on the cash flow statement for pre-development cash burn and the monetization of the 6,500 oz sulfide stockpile. Apart from that, focusing on Granite Creek’s WTP construction timeline and decline advance rates is also important. If i-80 Gold’s management confirms toll-milling costs are contained and Archimedes remains ahead of schedule, then high-risk bulls can harvest high implied volatility (through delta-neutral calendar spreads or through writing OTM cash-secured puts) without taking equity risk (directional).

Overall, i-80 Gold is a high-quality geological powerhouse trapped inside a heavily diluted financial structure. The stock is fairly valued at current levels until operational execution catches up to the large share count. To check the actionability of the thesis, I will keep a check on 3 forward monitorables. The first one is the regulatory approval timelines for Lone Tree's mercury abatement and air quality permits in early FY2026. The second one is Granite Creek's underground hydrology metrics post-commissioning of the 4,000 gpm WTP. Lastly, the third one is the accelerated Mineral Point feasibility study data. I will wait for definitive de-risking of the Lone Tree CapEx before initiating a buy.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: PSF: ~8% Yield On Bank Preferreds
data.description:
Do you have any preferred stocks in your portfolio? Preferreds can be a useful source of high-yield income. In addition, preferred dividends are usually more secure than common dividends, as they have stronger coverage and are at a higher rank in the creditor's pile.

Instead of parsing through scads of individual preferreds, some investors turn to Closed End Funds, "CEF's," to do the sorting for them.

One preferred-focused CEF is the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund, Inc. (PSF).

Fund Profile:

"The primary investment objective of the Fund is high current income through investment in preferred and other income securities. The secondary investment objective is capital appreciation." (PSF site)

Begun in 2010, PSF is a mid-range-size debt closed-end fund, or CEF, with 317 holdings and 37K in average daily volume. Management uses leverage, which was 33.31% as of 2/28/26. The Expense ratio of 3.76% includes 2.48% in interest expense.

Holdings:

As of 12/31/25, the portfolio contained 88% in Institutional Preferred Shares, 1% in Floating Rate Preferreds, and 11% in Retail Preferreds.

Banks are big issuers of preferred stocks; hence, PSF's 54% sector weighting in Banking is much heavier than its allocations to Utilities, at 11%; Insurance, at 10%; Pipelines, at 8%; and other sectors:

As of 12/31/25, the U.S. had the biggest national exposure, at 47%, followed by Canada, at 11%, and the UK and France, both at 9%, with other nations running from 3% for Spain to 4% for Switzerland.

Investment-Grade securities comprised 50% of PSF's holdings as of 12/31/25, with 37% in non-investment grade and 12% unrated:

The portfolio is skewed toward long-term maturities, with 14.27% in 20-30 years and 14.79% in 30+ years. There's also 3.37% in 10-15 year and 1.37% in 7-10 year maturities.

As noted earlier, the Banking industry dominates PSF's holdings - 8 out of 10 of its top 10 holdings are in the Banking sector, with familiar names such as Citigroup, Bank of America, and BNP Paribas. The top 10 holdings comprise just 10.3% of the portfolio.

Dividends:

PSF has paid $0.1260 monthly since July 2023. At $19.40, it yields 7.79% and goes ex-dividend next on 4/14/26, with a 4/30/26 pay date. Management already declared $0.1250 distributions for May and June.

Earnings:

NII rose 15.5% in the fiscal year ending 12/31/25, to $15.35, while Net Realized Gains fell to $6.85M, vs. $9.95M in 2024. Unrealized Appreciation also declined, from $9.18M to $2.93M.

NII + Realized Gains covered 2025 distributions by 1.22X in 2025 vs. 1.28X in 2024, a lower rate but still strong.

Net Assets increased by 2.7% in 2025, to ~$266M:

Taxes:

Management estimated that 9/30/25 to 2/27/26 distributions included ~3% Return of Capital, "ROC," and ~97% NII:

Performance:

PSF has outperformed its linked index and the ICE BofA Fixed Rate Preferred Index on a price and NAV basis over the past 1-year and 10-year periods and since inception. It also outperformed those indexes over the past 3-year period on an NAV basis.

PSF was roughly in line with the Morningstar US CEF Preferreds category in 2025 and outperformed it in 2023. It fell a bit further in price than the category in 2022, declining 24.6% vs. 22.47% for the category.

Risks:

Stock Market Risk: As many CEFs often trade at discounts to their NAVs, there's a risk that PSF will also decline over long or short periods.

Interest Rate Risk: As shown in the above chart, rising rates have a negative effect on PSF - it fell over 24% in 2022, a year of rising rates.

Valuations:

CEFs' daily valuations are calculated at the end of each trading day. Buying CEFs at deeper than historic discounts to NAV can be a useful strategy due to mean reversion. As of the 3/25/26 close, PSF was trading at a 9.34% discount to its daily NAV/share of $21.52. That's a deeper discount than its 1-, 3-, and 5-year average discounts of 7.26%, 6.49%, and 3.65%, respectively.

Parting Thoughts:

We rate PSF a Buy due to its deeper-than-average discount to NAV, its solid dividend coverage, and its positioning in preferred shares.

All tables are furnished by Hidden Dividend Stocks Plus, unless otherwise noted.

data.title: PepsiCo: Elliott's Catalyst + 4% Yield = The Defensive Buy Of 2026
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Investment Call: Strong Buy

The investment story of PepsiCo, Inc. (PEP) is one of the best-written investment stories in the entire large-cap consumer history. The current price of the stock is estimated at ~$150 per share in March 2026 -- near its 52-week low of $127.60 and well below its all-time closing high of $177.25 -- derated relative to its fundamental quality. Frito-Lay North America is experiencing a volume pressure that has caused the growth of organic revenue to 1.7% in fiscal 2025. Elliott Investment Management, holding a $4 billion activist stake, is leading a complicated strategic shift.

Three pillars of the grotesquely underestimated long-term case have never been replicated over any timeframe of American corporate history -- 54 consecutive years of rising per-share dividends. With an annualized rate of approximately 27% of the total revenues of the company, a rate of $5.69 per share ($1.4225 quarterly dividend × 4, in effect since June 2025; note: a forward rate of ~$5.92 takes effect from June 2026 per PepsiCo's February 4, 2026 dividend press release), and being one of the best in all the major categories of salty snacks, the Frito-Lay snack portfolio generates structurally high margins.

The restructuring programme, which will prove tough to execute in 2026 (according to the analysts), is meant to turn the brand portfolio and the cost base into more efficient forms by 2026 and 2027. The current yield on cost is already very competitive, with a target of 12 months to attain $185 prices and a core earnings multiple of just 22x forward earnings on FY2026E core EPS of approximately $8.65, supported by a dividend yield already at an attractive 3.9% on cost at current prices.

Three situation-specific catalysts support the investment thesis. The deal with Elliott Management would involve a cut of 20% in the SKUs of US products and specific price cuts in the core Frito-Lay brands, which activists consider a reliable measure in regaining margins. It is accompanied by the recovery of the volume. The experience that Elliott has with the other well-known consumer brands, including Starbucks, shows that the usage of its support can speed up the management improvement programs.

The $1.95 billion acquisition of Poppi and the $1.2 billion acquisition of Siete Foods have helped the portfolio to reposition strategically towards the highest-growing sub-segments of the market, including those that are better-for-you and functional beverages and snacks. Frito-Lay had a distribution chain that put them at a direct competitive advantage, which is also the area of the strongest structural tailwinds. PEP has changed to ~18.5x earnings, much less than its 5-year average of ~26.6x and compared to the S&P 500 forward multiple. It is a rare case where a Dividend King stock with a Morningstar wide-moat rating is turned into a Dividend King.

Q4 and Full-Year 2025 Results: Stabilisation and Sequential Acceleration

The announcement of the Q4 and overall 2025 earnings of PepsiCo on February 3rd, 2026, went against the overall trend, showing a positive sequential growth in the second half of the fiscal year. The estimated net revenue was $93.93 billion in the year 2025, and the increase was reported as 2.3% and organic as 1.7%. The full-year core (non-GAAP) EPS was $8.14, which matched the $8.16 printed a year ago, and only slightly lower than the management guidance range of December 2025.

The fourth quarter net revenue was +5.6% y-o-y, and the core EPS was $2.26, compared to the consensus of $2.24, and the core EPS was greater than the prior year low range of comparison, marking a 68% GAAP EPS increase in the quarter. This result triggered a significant recovery and had the effect of leading to a significant recovery in growth in the stock market, and the PEP stock rose by about 15% in the weeks following earnings, determining that the drop in peak volume had relaxed and that the approach taken by management of pricing and innovation had already begun to bear fruit.

The segment mix is a story of purposeful trade-offs. The Frito-Lay North America division, which is most scrutinized by investors, given its contribution to the overall profitability of the group, however, experienced a modest increase in net revenue to approximately $27.53 billion from $27.43 billion in 2024. Even though the current volume is five consecutive quarters lower than it was in the last year, the sequential changes in volume improved in Q4. In the earnings call, Elliott pressured the management team to lower prices by up to 15% on core brands, including Lay's, Doritos, Cheetos, and Tostitos - targeting a less-affluent consumer group that had moved up to their own-label alternatives and was also increasing innovation in portion control and healthier choices.

The net revenue of PepsiCo Beverages North America contributed $28.20 billion, and the growth of the beverage business was 4% organic, which was caused by zero-sugar reformulations and advancements at the outset occasioned by the Poppi distribution rollout. The performance of Europe/Middle East/Africa was quite remarkable, and the total generated value made $18.03 billion. In Q4, Latin America produced $10.55 billion, Asia Pacific/Australia/New Zealand/China regions earned $4.63 billion, and the international business incurred 27 basis points of core operating margin expansion in Q4.International Beverages Franchise contributed $5.00 billion in FY2025, up 2% year-over-year on solid 3% organic growth.

The balance sheet remains stable. As dividends rose by 4%, PepsiCo announced on February 3rd that it will produce more than $7 billion of free cash flow in fiscal 2025, which appeared to be sufficient. In 2026, another share buyback of $10 billion was approved until February 2030. The $1.4225 per share quarterly dividend rate of PepsiCo is currently worth $5.69 ($1.4225 × 4; note: a 4% annualized increase to ~$5.92 is set to take effect beginning June 2026 per PepsiCo's February 4, 2026 dividend press release) annually due to 54 consecutive annual per-share dividend increases --- a feat matched by fewer than 25 publicly traded companies in the globe. The company targets a record improvement in productivity by 2026 according to CEO Ramon Laguarta, which implies that the core operating margin gains of at least 100 basis points also encouraged by the role of Elliott can already be seen in the forward plan.

Frito-Lay: The Structural Moat the Market Is Discounting

Frito-Lay North America is not a commodity snack company on a cyclical downturn, but its structural advantages have been temporarily reduced by an overshoot on price. According to Morningstar's wide-moat research on PepsiCo, the best salty snacks in the United States are Lay's and Ruffles in potato chips, Doritos and Tostitos in tortilla chips, cheese snacks such as Cheetos, and Fritos in corn chips. Morningstar cites the fact that PepsiCo is strong because of its unmatched brand awareness, extensive supply chain coverage, and retailer relations that are functionally inimitable at point-of-sale.

A typical physical distribution platform, like the DSD network of Frito-Lay, offering direct-store-delivery services to some 200,000 retail outlets in the US, is a brick-and-mortar distribution system that has been built up over a period of years, and cannot be replicated by any competitor under any circumstances. PepsiCo's snack business, comprising Quaker Foods and international snacks, brings in most of its revenue and operating profits, with about 58% of it.

The pressure in terms of volume that Frito-Lay has been facing in recent times is real, yet explainable. PepsiCo and other major consumer packaged goods corporations raised the prices of their main snack brands by more than 15% between 2022 and 2024. The cumulative price increases stretched the affordability tolerance of the value-seeking consumer cohort. The resultant effect was that the private-label salty snack alternatives, which often cost 20--30% of their proprietary counterparts, took a large share of the shelf space and were also subjected to the consumer testing.

In its presentation during the Consumer Analyst Group of New York (CAGNY) conference in February 2026, PepsiCo provided data on the volume weakness being the key issue and its 2026 strategy of reducing prices on a few of its hero SKUs, as outlined in the presentation of the company. This mechanism is associated with the direct goal of using the savings in cost identified by Elliott in advertising. Elliott's 20% rationalization of all US product SKUs is a key operational move in the process of lowering per-unit costs and raising retailer fill rates, creating a virtuous cycle that supports both gross margins and volume recovery.

The sustainability of the Frito-Lay opportunity in the long term is anchored on the structural or cyclical demographic and societal trends. The increase in the global consumption of snacks per capita will be achieved through the growth of PepsiCo, which will be involved in the development of non-alcoholic drinks, in developing markets. PepsiCo has purchased a popular Mexican-American food company called Siete Foods with a large following, which is valued at $1.2 billion in late 2025. The introduction of healthier chips and tortillas is due to the variety of consumers in the US. With the acquisition of Poppi, Frito-Lay can now claim to have functional drinks in its portfolio, which is growing at an annual rate exceeding 20% and a shift towards healthier diets as consumers reduce their use of traditional sodas. The measures implemented by PepsiCo indicate that it is a multi-level plan to regain its status as the best snack brand in the coming decade.

The Dividend Machine: 54 Consecutive Annual Increases and Still Accelerating

A company that has been increasing its dividends per share over 50 consecutive years would have a higher level of performance compared to other investments in the long term, termed as Dividend King companies. In the world, there are just approximately 25 companies analogous to PepsiCo. The annualised dividend rate of $5.69 (current; rising to ~$5.92 from June 2026) per share has grown by 4% by June 2026, and this has been a consistent pattern over the years of compound growth, which has happened across the five decades regardless of economic fluctuation -- the incidence of recession and a market environment in five years, adverse macroeconomic conditions, and technology. PEP has had its best covered dividend yield in over a decade, with a current share price of around $150 and a trailing yield of 3.9%. The trough of June 2025, when the shares went down to $127.60, was a momentary phenomenon. The historical dividend CAGR of approximately 7.4% generated during the last 10 years suggests that the forward yield-on-cost will be more than 5% when growth rates remain fairly low (4-5% p.a.).

The sustainability aspect of dividends is worth considering. Under FY2025, PepsiCo will have approximately $7 billion of free cash flow, and the current annualised dividend commitment is approximated at around $8.1 billion, while all other factors will remain unchanged. The share buyback program, which is valued at $10 billion, will strengthen the belief of the management and the board in the capability of the business to produce sustainable cash flows. The core EPS dividend payout ratio remains below 75%, which is deemed a stable and sustainable level for PepsiCo. The PepsiCo dividend has gone through various incidents over the years, including the 2008 financial crisis, and the COVID shock of spring 2020. The management team and board consider the dividend as non-negotiable even with the inflation projected in 2022-2024. Contrary to a pure growth stock, PEP is supported by dividends reinvested in case of price weakness -- precisely the situation that currently presents itself.

Share Price Context: A Rare Entry Point After a Multi-Year De-Rating

Because of the broad analyst consensus downgrades - with average price targets clustering around the $160-$165 range (per Benzinga analyst ratings) - in the early 2025, the share price of PEP has been affected by the consensus downgrades and has dropped to the trough of $127.60 in June. Elliott's intervention signalled operational concerns, driving a broader rotation away from defensive consumer names toward AI infrastructure, followed by a recovery to $171 following the Q4 earnings beat in February 2026. The risk aversion in the market led to a decline to around $150 in March 2026. The net effect is that the stock is trading at a substantial discount to its present and past highs, whereas the underlying trend has shot up considerably since the lows in June. According to WallStreetZen's DCF model, the PEP price will probably attain its intrinsic value with a positive discounted cash flow (DCF model) in consideration of the existing value of about $243 per share. The assumption that price is under by DCF basis term is obvious, although overestimation of assumptions is also determined. It correctly captures the directionality of discount vs. quality.

Coca-Cola (KO) is a powerful competitor of PEP, so it is an appropriate place of entry to value it. Although the aggregate base of its revenue is greater than the aggregate base of its absolute share of ~$47 billion, KO has an almost 23.5x forward earnings on PEP's ~18.5x. The product mix of the company is more diversified (snacks and beverages) than that of Coca-Cola (beverages only), and the long-term growth of the dividend profile is similar, and it may be more beneficial. Although the delta has decreased since 2023, KO's light structure and pure-play beverage offer have traditionally been pricier than PEP. Our initiation report has found that there are certain growth levers in PEP, however, even in the event that we were only successful in half closing the forward P/E gap between PEP and KO, the same is not clear. That would imply at least a share price of over $185, and, hence, higher than our current price target, but an EPS increase would not be anticipated in the prevailing market environment.

FY2026 Guidance: Volume Recovery, Margin Expansion, and Portfolio Reinvestment

PepsiCo's fiscal 2026 guidance, affirmed on February 3, 2026, and the growth of organic revenue was 4-6%, and net revenue growth was approximated at approximately $97--99 billion in net revenue at midpoint. The central constant currency expansion of 5-7% (or 7-9% excluding global minimum tax) and the concentration of core operating margin improvement of at least 100 basis points on core operating margin. These goals are a tremendous increment on the 1.7% organic growth in fiscal 2025, and the management has given an operational breakdown of each outlet pull. They also hope to win the additional shelf space and ease the marketing of the same products by pricing their core SKUs lower and taking advantage of the Poppi distribution build-up. Beverages North America will launch additional brands of zero-sugar carbonated soft drinks like Poppi. The company is promoting global trade, as they realize that there is a possibility of distribution in Latin America and Asia Pacific.

The confidence is demonstrated by management announcing a $10 billion share buyback program in Q4. PepsiCo has a market capitalisation of approximately $205 billion, which means that it might need to buy back approximately 4.9% of its outstanding shares within its four-year tenure under a full repurchase programme. Even when the growth of revenue is less than the low-single-digit range, per-share earnings and dividend payouts are impacted. With its buyback and dividend growth commitment, PEP is projected to produce more than $10 billion in total cash returns in fiscal 2026, and it will be one of the largest capital return vehicles in the S&P 500.

Risk Factors That Merit Consideration

GLP-1 Weight-Loss Drug Structural Headwind

The main victims of a possible change in structural demand caused by GLP-1 weight-loss drugs such as Ozempic and Zepbound will be salty snack companies, among them PepsiCo. Using research, a number of investment banks modelled scenarios that take into account the reduction of caloric consumption and snacking by drug-treated consumers. PepsiCo has revealed this risk to its investors. The current trends in volume indicate that the adoption of GLP-1 is strongly driven by the richest consumers, who are already ready to pay a premium for highly priced snack brands. The wider market is yet to be driven by the consumers who place their values on non-pharmacological intervention rather than cost. Investors have not fully priced in the risk, which should be monitored by the PEP investors.

Volume Recovery Execution Risk

Frito-Lay North America has had five consecutive quarters of volume decreases, and the effectiveness of this recovery approach will be of utmost importance in terms of fiscal 2026 directions. The Elliott deal price cuts are needed but not vital because volume can only be maintained when consumers adopt product innovations, simplified SKU approach is well executed through the DSD network, among other considerations. Consumers have the option of a branded snack that has a good spending environment, yet the possibility of growth remains. The organic growth of revenue of +4--6% is extremely dangerous should macroeconomic factors become worse or should price cuts fail to result in volume recovery at the rate the management projects.

Emerging Market Currency and Geopolitical Exposure

The core of the PepsiCo income is the company's non-U.S. businesses, especially in Latin America, Mexico, Brazil, Middle East, and Asia Pacific. Because of the difficulties caused by currency translations and geopolitical interruptions, including alterations in the U.S. tariff policy and the spillover effects of regional conflicts, earnings cannot be hedged wholly. The company is also basing its spot rate to identify a foreign exchange tailwind of about 0.5--1 percentage point in fiscal 2026 that may vary as the dollar changes.

Bull and Bear Cases

Two of the three catalysts --- price changes and a smaller portfolio that targets low-end shoppers, and volume recovery --- are credited with the bull case in fiscal 2026 and 2027. Cost moderation combined with an increase in margin Poppi/Siete contribution will produce an improvement in gross margin, although multiple re-rating is requisite due to the Elliott restructuring and the earnings leverage. Even in the years that it has been a Dividend King, a price target of $185--$200 per share with a multiple of 21--22x remains on the conservative side of the argument. When a dividend yield is incorporated, the total return of the bull case in two years is above 40%.

The case of the Bear is defined by a GLP-1 structural narrative that is more disruptive than expected, and is a volume recovery slowed down because the Elliott restructuring causes operational disruption that impairs volume recovery. The fact that the sales of snacks have dropped following the economic crisis limits the capabilities of PepsiCo to increase prices. The organic growth is tapped at 1--2%, the growth of EPS is nonexistent, and the stock re-rates to 16--17x earnings that may reach $135--$140, as the investors do not believe the dividend growth commitment is sustainable. In this bear case, it will require a major change in the historical trend, but PepsiCo has demonstrated its capacity to sustain the dividend in harsh economies.

Conclusion: The Case for Strong Buy

PepsiCo is not a growth stock. To some investors, it is the 9th inning, two on one out, lifetime opportunity, a compounding engine with a 54-year dividend growth track record, an inherently structurally unbeatable business model, and an exceptionally high-quality snack-and-beverage product. Elliott Investment Management is a clear-cut shareholder-value catalyst. PEP no longer trades at a price some have termed as being slightly underpriced. It is projected to have an annual yield of 3.9% with a forecasted growth rate of 4--7% in the next 5 fiscal years and a multi-year discount to the intrinsic value of the stock, which forms an asymmetric risk/reward profile appealing to patient long-term investors. The short-term clatter, consisting of Frito-Lay sentimentality, messy appearance of activist action, and macro sensitivity on the global stage, is a reality. Rotation of institutions bearing a given name is precipitated by the intricacy that has provided a point of entry to patient capital.

The Frito-Lay franchise is now seen as one of the most defensible consumer brands across the world. The direct-store-delivery network (over 200,000 retail locations) is unrivalled. The annualised dividend of $5.69 (rising to ~$5.92 from June 2026) per share is not an expense, but a tool for gaining confidence in the free cash flow. The 2026 reorganization plan is future-oriented and will require a short-term outlay of capital but will create a low-cost operation capable of producing compounded returns even after several years of the current guide. Our price target of $185 reflects a 22x multiple on $8.65 in FY2026E core EPS. We believe that the premium-multiple option of a wide-moat Dividend King would be conservative, as the total return target over the next year is ~27% as a result of price growth and existing dividend yield. Strong Buy.

data.title: ADMA Biologics: Differentiated Plasma Approach With Asymmetric Upside Potential (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
ADMA Biologics, Inc. (ADMA) is a biologics company that produces immune globulin (IG) treatments. These products are helpful for treating immune deficiencies and for infectious disease indications. The company remains a small player in a large IG market, and it expects to expand its market share through its differentiated ASCENIV franchise and by growing its high-titer plasma supply network. Recently, ADMA faced scrutiny after a short-seller report challenged the quality of its reported 2025 growth. However, ADMA has rejected those claims. In my view, my initial bull thesis remains valid but at an even more compelling entry price today.

Asymmetric Upside On IG Products

ADMA Biologics, Inc., is a commercial biopharmaceutical company that develops biologics from plasma, especially immune globulin treatments for patients with immune deficiencies or at risk due to infectious diseases. ADMA was founded back in 2004 and is currently headquartered in Ramsey, New Jersey. ADMA has a manufacturing campus in Boca Raton, Florida, and its BioCenters subsidiary is headquartered in Cary, North Carolina. Additionally, ADMA’s biocenters are FDA-licensed facilities where people donate plasma. I previously covered ADMA last August, but since then, the stock has declined by 43.6%, which is why I thought it was worthwhile revisiting this name.

As a quick recap, ADMA’s portfolio includes three FDA-approved products: ASCENIV, BIVIGAM, and Nabi-HB. ASCENIV and BIVIGAM are intravenous immune globulin (IVIG). ASCENIV is indicated for Primary Immunodeficiency Disease (PIDD) in adults and adolescents aged 12 to 17. These are made using a patented donor-screening and plasma-pooling methodology, including plasma with elevated antibodies against Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV).

Having said that, BIVIGAM is more of a broad-antibody IVIG that contains antibodies that are similar to human plasma. BIVIGAM is indicated for PIDD in adults and children aged 2 and older. Moreover, Nabi-HB is a hyperimmune globulin rich in antibodies against the hepatitis B virus. Nabi-HB is made from plasma donated by people who were previously vaccinated against this disease. This compound is indicated for post-exposure protection to reduce the risk of contracting the illness.

Additionally, ADMA’s pipeline includes the candidate SG-001, which is being studied in the preclinical stages. It’s a Streptococcus pneumoniae immune globulin (IG) that contains high levels of antibodies against this pathogen, which is the cause of infections in the lung, sinus, ear, bloodstream, and meningitis, especially in vulnerable people. This IG is also made from human plasma.

With that in mind, ADMA’s biocenters collect hyperimmune and normal source plasma. The company uses its own collection infrastructure and also third-party suppliers, especially for plasma with high levels of antibodies (high-titer) against specific targets for specialty IG production. The third-party agreements secure high-titer plasma supply into the late 2030s and provide access to more than 280 third-party collection centers, involving four counterparties, including Grifols and KEDPlasma.

According to the company, the IG market is large and growing to treat Primary Immunodeficiency (PI), and it is still an unmet need even though IG products have been used for decades. Some PI patients on standard IVIG still develop problems such as respiratory tract infections, bronchiectasis, and chronic lung disease because the standard treatment does not fully solve the existing problems, so there is still space for differentiated therapies.

This global plasma fractionation market could reach around $58.2 billion by 2030, and ADMA is still a small player. There are other much larger competitors, such as Takeda (TAK), CSL (CSL), Grifols (GRFS), Octapharma, and BPL, a company part of Kedrion, which have 97% of the market. Thus, I do believe there’s ample room for future growth with ADMA’s approach to IG.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, ADMA currently trades at a $2.2 billion market cap. Their latest 10-K report shows its balance sheet holds $87.6 million in cash against $72.1 million in long-term debt. Based on those figures, I estimate ADMA’s EV at approximately $2.2 billion as well. And, their business is projected to generate $778.5 million in revenues by 2027, according to analysts.

Therefore, that would price the stock at a reasonable forward EV/S of 2.8. For comparison, ADMA’s peers’ median forward EV/S is 3.3, so the stock already looks cheap from this angle. In fact, I was bullish on ADMA back in August 2025, and the stock traded at a $4.4 billion valuation at the time. So that means the stock is now at a 50% discount since then, and I don’t see any meaningful deterioration in the business to justify such a steep pullback.

If anything, management is guiding them to further improvements on their adjusted-EBITDA margins, which showcases that their business is likely strengthening, not deteriorating, as the stock price might suggest. Moreover, assuming ADMA’s topline reaches $778.5 million as projected, it could imply they’ll be generating $459.3 million in adjusted-EBITDA next year. That also implies a cheap forward EV/EBITDA of 4.8, which is compelling in its own right. But, compared to its sector’s median forward EV/EBITDA of 11.0, it highlights just how depressed ADMA’s valuation is today.

Lastly, I think it’s worth mentioning that the company is also cash flow positive. I estimate they generated $27.8 million in positive free cash flow during 2025. Note that I got that figure by simply adding ADMA’s 2025 cash flows from operations and CAPEX. But more importantly, it corroborates that they’re self-sustainable despite having to support their ongoing revenue ramp-up. Naturally, the stock is not without its risks, and there’s a short report on ADMA that may weigh on investors’ sentiment in the near term as well. Fortunately, ADMA has provided a rebuttal to that short thesis. And I think it’s worthwhile reading both sides of the argument before initiating a bull or bear position at this point.

Aside from that, I noticed ADMA’s revenues are highly concentrated on just two customers. Almost 73% of ADMA’s consolidated revenues come from Biocare and CuraScript. This dynamic inherently exposes ADMA to substantial downside if it were to lose either of those clients. I also imagine it weakens ADMA’s negotiating power, which could be a headwind to their margins in the long run. But, since management itself is actually guiding to better margins in the coming years, I don’t think this is a major risk for now, at least.

Similarly, if ASCENIV or BIVIGAM faces regulatory setbacks or clinical concerns with patients, the stock could very well trend lower. For example, we saw that ADMA voluntarily withdrew 3 BIVIGAM lots, which showcases that faulty products are always a risk with this type of business. And if faulty products reach patients, it can always cause lawsuits, regulatory holds, loss of trust, health damages, etc. But again, the fact that ADMA caught those lots on time and proactively addressed the issue gives me confidence in their ability to execute going forward.

Conclusion: “Strong Buy” Upgrade

Overall, I was already bullish on ADMA back in 2025, mostly due to its promising growth prospects and improving margins. Since then, the numbers have supported my initial bull case, as revenue has indeed increased with margins projected to improve in the coming years. Interestingly, the stock is substantially cheaper today, so I feel the risk-reward has only improved for bulls at these levels. Therefore, I believe an upgrade to “Strong Buy” makes sense because ADMA’s depressed valuation multiples offer asymmetric upside potential. Just make sure you’re fully aware of the potential risks I previously mentioned before fully committing to a long-term bullish position.

data.title: April 2026 Monthly
data.description:
Wars rarely stay where they start. The Israeli and American military campaign against Iran - the most direct confrontation with Tehran in decades - has sent shockwaves well beyond the Middle East. The fog of war obscures the tactical picture almost daily, but the economic transmission mechanisms are clear enough, and investors who mistake this for a regional skirmish over energy do so at their peril.

One-Two Punch

The first punch is inflationary. Iran is not a bit player in global commodity markets. It sits atop some of the world's most consequential supply chains - not just oil and gas, which command the headlines, but sulfur and urea, which quietly underpin the global food system. Sulfur is a critical feedstock for phosphate fertilizers. Disrupt that supply, and you are not merely raising the price of a barrel of crude; you are raising the price of wheat in Egypt, corn in Brazil, and bread in Lagos.

Energy markets feel the shock first and most visibly, but the agricultural commodity complex is where the second-order damage accumulates, slowly and with devastating effect on the world's most food-insecure populations. Industrial commodities, like aluminum and helium (essential for semiconductor fabrication), have also been disrupted.

The second punch destroys demand. That is the cruel arithmetic of commodity shocks. The same price surge that generates windfall revenues for producers squeezes household budgets everywhere else, sapping the consumer spending that drives global growth. It boosts input costs for many manufacturers, and corporate margins compress. Capital expenditure plans go on hold.

The very inflation that appears to signal economic heat is, in this context, a leading indicator of demand destruction. Central banks find themselves in the most uncomfortable of positions: facing prices that argue for tightening while growth signals argue for caution. It is stagflation's calling card, and it arrived before most were ready to receive it.

Monetary Policy Adjustments

While market participants seem to be inclined to expect a relatively short war on Iran, the pendulum of interest rate expectations swung dramatically. There was a clear bias towards expecting the easing cycle to continue before the war began. The market was sure of Bank of England and Federal Reserve rate cuts; it was less confident but retained a bias toward the European Central Bank and the Bank of Canada cutting rates themselves.

The swaps market was more extreme, but even now it is discounting around a 65% chance of a hike at the ECB's next meeting at the end of April. Three rate hikes are fully discounted for this year, and there is about a 20% chance of a fourth.

The swaps market has a little less than a 60% chance of a Bank of England hike when they too meet on April 30. Three hikes are fully discounted for this year and a little bit more. On the eve of the war, the market had two cuts fully discounted, and a small chance of a third.

Before the war, the futures market was pricing in two rate cuts by the Federal Reserve and a 40% chance of a third. It is now discounting slightly more than a 60% chance of a hike by the end of the year.

From Trade Wars to Kinetic Wars

There is a larger context that deserves attention. The United States has now bombed at least seven countries since the start of last year. Whatever one's view of the strategic rationale in any individual case, the cumulative picture is of a rules-based international order in accelerating dissolution.

The institutions and norms that once provided a framework for managing conflict - however imperfectly - are being bypassed with increasing frequency and diminishing apology. This is not a temporary disruption. It is a structural shift in the geopolitical environment in which all economic activity takes place. Risk premiums that were compressed during decades of relative stability need to be repriced, and that repricing is neither linear nor orderly.

The truth is said to be the first casualty in a war, but it was already challenged before the first strike. Battle damage assessments are contested. Escalation ladders are unpredictable. A drone strike, a tanker seizure, a miscalculation in the Strait of Hormuz - any of these can move markets in minutes with information that takes days to verify. Volatility is not a momentary condition to be waited out; it is the operating environment, and it demands to be managed accordingly.

Trusted Advisers

Which brings us to the only actionable conclusion available in moments like this: discipline matters more, not less, when the news is loudest. Diversification, position sizing, stop-loss discipline, and a clear-eyed assessment of tail risks are not optional refinements for calm markets - they are the minimum requirements for navigating turbulent ones.

The instinct to act decisively on incomplete information is precisely what fog-of-war conditions seem to tempt. The antidote is a disciplined process, a reliable framework, and trusted advisers who have seen cycles before. In markets shaped by forces this large and this fast-moving, the quality of your decision-making process is the only edge you can reliably control.

Bannockburn World Currency Index

Bannockburn's World Currency Index, a GDP-weighted basket of the currencies of the dozen largest economies, snapped a four-month advance and posted its biggest decline (about 1.25%) since last July. The decline reflected the fact that all of the components of the index fell against the dollar. In fact, the decline brought BWCI back toward the lows for the year recorded before recovering near mid-January.

Among the G10 currencies, sterling fared the best, losing "only" about 1.6%. It may have been supported by the rate expectations adjusted in the UK, which was the most extreme. Before the war began, the market's disappointment with the UK economic data saw the two-year yield approach 3.5%, the lowest since August 2024. At its peak on March 23, it briefly traded above 4.70%. The Canadian dollar fell by about 1.8%, making it the second-best performer among the G10 components.

The Australian dollar was the worst G10 component in the index. It fell by about 3.5% in March, giving back around half of what it had gained in the first two months of the year, which had put it atop the G10. The Australian dollar was helped by the two rate hikes and hawkish tone the central bank delivered. The euro and yen declined by about 2.5-2.6%.

Among the emerging market currencies in Bannockburn's World Currency Index, the Chinese yuan's 0.70% decline was the best performer. The relative stability of the yuan and Chinese interest rates may be encouraging another look by global asset managers. The Brazilian real was in second place with a little more than a 2.2% decline. The others lost more than 4%, with the Russian ruble off around 5.5%. Although the US relaxed a restriction on Russian oil, and Moscow is charging a premium for it over Brent (before the war it was at a discount), the war with Ukraine has intensified. Ukraine reportedly struck an important refinery (Kirishi) in Leningrad as well as other Russian oil processing and industrial facilities in the Baltic region.

Bannockburn's World Currency Index reached its best level since September 2024, around 92.25 in mid-February, before falling in March. Escalation of the conflict could push the index another 0.75%-1.0% lower that would bring it to around last April's lows. A ceasefire could see the BWCI back to the mid-February high. A new high would lend credence to our medium- and longer-term bearish outlook for the dollar.

U.S. Dollar: The dollar was recovering from the sell-off it incurred when the US was pressing hard on Greenland, but the war on Iran gave it new impetus. Part of it seemed driven by position adjustment, like the unwinding of carry trades and foreign investors buying back short dollar hedges on equity investments. The US is seen as more able to deal with the energy shock than Europe, and this may have encouraged some genuine safe haven demand for dollars. Although there has been a dramatic adjustment in expectations for the Federal Reserve, it has generally been less than in many other countries. Yet, in the first instance, the surge in US rates is supportive of the dollar, even though interest rate differentials have moved against it. Each time the market grasps the faintest of hopes of a short-term war, the dollar is sold off as risk is embraced. The swaps market is discounting a much higher chance that the European Central Bank, the Bank of England, and the Bank of Japan will lift rates in April than the Federal Reserve. The investigation into the Federal Reserve has not formally ended, and this is holding up the confirmation process of Powell's successor, Warsh. There is precedent for Powell, who is also a governor (until 2028), to retain the post until a successor is confirmed. Meanwhile, it is possible that the complaint against the administration's 10% tariff under Section 122 of the 52-year-old trade act is heard by the US Court of International Trade. Regardless of the outcome, the eventual decision will be appealed. The 150-day limit on Section 122 legislation, without a congressional-authorized extension, ends on July 24.

Euro: In addition to fraying the alliance some more, the US-Israel war on Iran posed a new energy shock for Europe, and the euro was sold to seven-month lows in the middle of March near $1.1400. Brent crude oil rose to a decade-high premium over the US benchmark, WTI. The euro was a little above $1.18 before the war began. The ECB stood pat in March, but the hawks insist that a hike cannot be ruled out as soon as the next meeting at the end of April. The swaps market has about a 60% chance discounted for that meeting. Before the war began, the market had around a 55% chance of a cut this year and now has three hikes fully discounted and about a 10% chance of a fourth hike. This seems excessive. The ECB's updated forecasts recognize the two-way risk. The new economic projections shaved growth this year to 0.9% (from 1.2%) and next year's growth to 1.3% (from 1.4%). The inflation outlook worsened. The updated forecast is for 2.6% CPI this year (from 1.9%) and 2.0% next year (from 1.8%). Part of the ECB's reaction function may depend on how the governments respond. ECB President Lagarde explicitly called for governments to limit energy assistance to temporary and targeted efforts.

(As of March 27, indicative closing prices, previous in parentheses)

Spot: $1.1509 ($1.1812) Median Bloomberg One-month forecast: $1.1607 ($1.1900) One-month forward: $1.1525 ($1.1832) One-month implied vol: 7.8% (5.9%)

Japanese Yen: The dollar rose to almost JPY160 last month, its highest level since July 2024. The war had a more muted effect on Japanese markets than it did in Europe or the United States. The 10-year yield rose a little more than 25 bp and the two-year yield rose by around 17 basis points. Before the war began, the swaps market was pricing in about 44 bp of tightening this year and is now discounting about 56 bp. The Bank of Japan's next meeting concludes on April 28. Given the upward revision in Q4 25 GDP (1.3% at an annualized rate from 0.2%) and the strong start of this year (January industrial production rose by 4.3%, more than in 2025, and a 1.7% increase in tertiary activity, which was also more than last year), we suspect the odds of a BOJ rate hike in April are more than the 69% chance being discounted by the swaps market. The largest labor union federation secured a preliminary increase for average pay of 5.26%, topping 5% for the third consecutive year. The dollar reached JPY160.30 on March 27, the first breach of JPY160 since 2024, when it got to almost JPY162. Returning there seems a strong possibility. Bank of Japan and the Ministry of Finance officials seem more sophisticated than drawing a fixed intervention line at JPY160. It has been a broad dollar advance, and the greenback's rise against the yen has been orderly. The yen's 2.5% loss puts it near the middle of the performance of G10 currencies since the war began. It has not simply been a one-way market. One-month implied volatility is firm, mostly chopping between 9% and 10% this month, well off the January peak near 11.5%.

Spot: JPY160.31 (JPY156.05) Median Bloomberg One-month forecast: JPY158.52 (JPY154.00) One-month forward: JPY159.90 (JPY155.65). One-month implied vol: 9.9% (9.2%)

British Pound: The British economy was struggling before the war began, and the fiscal headroom was limited. The economy unexpectedly stagnated in January. Industrial production contracted, and services output was flat. Before the war began, the swaps market was convinced that the Bank of England would cut rates at least twice this year, with around an 80% chance of a cut in March. The spike in energy prices caused a violent shift in the market's outlook. The BOE stood pat at last month's meeting, and some members of the Monetary Policy Committee seemed open to a rate hike. The next meeting is at the end of April, and the swaps market has about a 50% chance of a hike discounted and almost a 75% chance of at least two more hikes this year. The two-year yield jumped more than 100 bp last month. Despite a small pullback, it is still up around 95 bp. This seems to be excessive. And to be sure, sterling found little comfort from the surge in rates. After posting a February high near $1.3715, sterling overshot the $1.3250-$1.3300 target we suggested and found support closer to $1.3220. To confirm a bottom is in place, sterling needs to re-establish a foothold above the $1.3465 area, while a break of $1.3180-$1.3200 could encourage a move toward $1.3000-50.

Spot: $1.3259 ($1.3482) Median Bloomberg One-month forecast: $1.3306 ($1.3600) One-month forward: $1.3260 ($1.3480) One-month implied vol: 8.4% (7.2%)

Canadian Dollar: In the generally firmer US dollar environment in March, the Canadian dollar was among the best performers in the G10. It lost about 1.65%, only bested by the British pound. We think that the conventional narrative often exaggerates the link to oil prices. In fact, the rolling 30-day correlation between changes in WTI and the US dollar's movement against the Canadian dollar has swung from inverse to slightly positive since the middle of March, for the first time since early last November. Our alternative explanation is the Canadian dollar moves in the US orbit and in a rising greenback environment does relatively better, and the opposite is also true. We note that the volatility of the exchange rate is typically the lowest among the major currencies. The swaps market had been discounting about a 45% chance of a cut by the Bank of Canada before the war began but is now pricing in two hikes fully and about a 50% chance of a third hike. While Bank of Canada Governor Macklem was adamant that the central bank would not allow prices to fuel persistent inflation, he recognized that, unlike the energy shock in 2022, the economy is soft. In February, Canada lost the most jobs in four years. The Canadian economy has been rattled by a drop in exports and business investments, spurred by the shift in US policy. The US is engaging in separate talks with Mexico and Canada over the USMCA, with Prime Minister Carney looking to diversify its trading partners. The US dollar has risen above the downtrend line drawn off the late November 2025, January 2026, and mid-March 2026 highs. The mid-January high, near CAD1.3930, may be the next target, and then the CAD1.40 area beckons.

Spot: CAD1.3893 (CAD1.3650) Median Bloomberg One-month forecast: CAD1.3795 (CAD1.3700) One-month forward: CAD1.3875 (CAD1.3630) One-month implied vol: 5.2% (4.9%)
